english: <font face='$HandwrittenFont'> If you're reading this, run. Get back in your boat and row as far and as fast as you can. There is no safe harbor here, there is no treasure worth the horror that awaits you. It's a miracle you aren't dead already. Haldyn sent us from Japhet's Folly out into the sea, looking for hidden islands and forgotten coves. He said we needed outposts all around Tamriel to hide all the treasure we're soon to pillage. I say he's mad. A fresh group like us has more immediate concerns than outposts. We don't even have a name yet. But I kept my mouth shut, my head down, and did what I was told. I wish to the Nine that I'd have said something... This island looked so peaceful, so remote. Seemed like a good place for an outpost. Bjord and I stayed behind while the others scouted about. It wasn't long before we heard the screams. We peered over the rocks and I can't even describe what we saw, although I'll never forget it. The blood. Gods, the blood... What those monsters did brought out the coward in Bjord. All it did to me was raise a fire in my chest, a hate in my gut so strong I can almost feel it coursing into the mace in my hand. I told Bjord to run, to tell the others never to come here. I stayed to write out this warning to anyone unlucky enough to land on this rock. Now that I've done that duty, there's only one thing left. I'm going to kill as many of those things as I can. I'm even going to try for the big one. Maybe I'll do it. Maybe I'll kill every one of them. Maybe you've found this rock abandoned and safe. I doubt it... </font>
chinese: <font face='$HandwrittenFont'> 如果你读到这东西，快跑。回你自己的船上去能跑多快跑多快。这里不是避风港，也没有什么你值得忍受的恐惧。你到现在还没死真是个奇迹。 哈尔丁派我们从贾菲特之愚出海，寻找隐秘的海岛和洞窟。他说我们需要在泰姆瑞尔周围各处设立哨站来藏匿我们抢来的财宝。要我说他就是疯了。像我们这样的新组织有比哨站更紧迫的问题。我们到现在还没起名字呢。 但我还是闭了嘴低了头，按吩咐照办了。九圣灵在上，我真该说些什么的…… 这个岛看起来够平静，也够偏僻。应该是设哨站的好地方。布约尔德和我殿后，其他人则出去侦察四周。我们在石头后面盯着，我真没法形容看到了什么，但我永远也忘不了。血。天呐，全是血…… 那些怪物的所作所为让布约尔德的懦弱暴露了。但我只是感觉怒火中烧，内心的仇恨激荡，我甚至能感觉到它在我的钉锤上流动。我告诉布约尔德赶快跑，去告诉其他人不要到这里来。我要留下来写下这篇警告，提醒那些不幸来到这块石头的人。 现在既然我事情办完了，就剩下一件事了。我要尽我所能多杀点怪物。我甚至想试试那头大个儿的。 也许我能杀几个。也许我能把他们都宰了，然后你发现这块被遗弃的石头很安全。但我没什么把握…… </font>

English: <font face='$HandwrittenFont'> If you're reading this, run. Get back in your boat and row as far and as fast as you can. There is no safe harbor here, there is no treasure worth the horror that awaits you. It's a miracle you aren't dead already. Haldyn sent us from Japhet's Folly out into the sea, looking for hidden islands and forgotten coves. He said we needed outposts all around Tamriel to hide all the treasure we're soon to pillage. I say he's mad. A fresh group like us has more immediate concerns than outposts. We don't even have a name yet. But I kept my mouth shut, my head down, and did what I was told. I wish to the Nine that I'd have said something... This island looked so peaceful, so remote. Seemed like a good place for an outpost. Bjord and I stayed behind while the others scouted about. It wasn't long before we heard the screams. We peered over the rocks and I can't even describe what we saw, although I'll never forget it. The blood. Gods, the blood... What those monsters did brought out the coward in Bjord. All it did to me was raise a fire in my chest, a hate in my gut so strong I can almost feel it coursing into the mace in my hand. I told Bjord to run, to tell the others never to come here. I stayed to write out this warning to anyone unlucky enough to land on this rock. Now that I've done that duty, there's only one thing left. I'm going to kill as many of those things as I can. I'm even going to try for the big one. Maybe I'll do it. Maybe I'll kill every one of them. Maybe you've found this rock abandoned and safe. I doubt it... </font>

Even though everything seemed serene (the last embers crackling in the hearth; young servant girl and her child slumbering in a chair by the door; a tapestry half-finished against the wall, waiting to be completed tomorrow; one of the moons visible through a milky cloud outside the window; a lone bird, out of sight in the rafters, cooing placidly), Tay heard the first chords of the Song strike dissonantly somewhere far away. The bird in the rafters croaked and took flight through the window. The baby in the girl's arms woke and began to scream. The Song swelled in intensity, yet still remained subtle and stately in tempo. The movement of everything seemed to take on the rhythm of the music as if strange choreography had been staged: the girl rising to the window, the clouds reflecting back red from the inferno below, her scream, all muted, consumed by the Song. Everything that came thereafter Tay had seen so many times, it had almost ceased to be a nightmare. He did not remember anything of his life before coming to the island of Gorne, but he understood that there was something different in his past that set him apart from his cousins. It wasn't simply that his parents were dead. His cousin Baynarah's parents had also died in the War. Nor were the other Housemen on Gorne or nearby Mournhold unusually cruel to him. They treated him with the same polite indifference that any Indoril has for every other eight-year-old boy that got underfoot. But somehow, with absolutely certainty, Tay knew he was alone. Different. Because of a Song he always heard, and his nightmares. "You're certainly imaginative," his aunt Ulliah would smile patiently, before waving him away so she could return to her scriptures and chores. "Different? Everyone in the world thinks they're 'different,' that's what makes it such a common sentiment," said his older cousin Kalkorith who was studying to be Temple priest and had a firm grasp on paradoxes. "If you tell anyone else that you keep hearing music where there's no music to be heard, they'll call you mad and bury you in the Shrine of Sheogorath," his uncle Triffith would snarl, before striding away to attend his business. Only his nursemaid Edebah would listen to him seriously, and just nod with a faint look of pride. But she would never say another word. His cousin and chief playmate Baynarah was by far the least interested in the stories of his Song and his dreams. "How tiresome you are with all this, Tay," said Baynarah, after luncheon the summer of his eighth year. He, she, and a younger cousin Vaster walked into a clearing in the midst of flowering trees. The grass was very low, barely up to their ankles, and there were big black piles of leaves from the previous autumn. "Now, shall we get back to it? What shall we play?" Tay thought for a moment. "We could play the Siege of Orsinium." "What's that?" asked Vaster, their constant companion, three years their junior. "Orsinium was the home of the orcs, off in the Wrothgarian Mountains. For hundreds of years, it kept growing bigger and bigger and bigger. The orcs would come down out of the mountains and rape and pillage all over High Rock. And then, King Joile of Daggerfall and Gaiden Shinji of the Order of Diagna and someone else, I forget, from Sentinel all joined together against Orsinium. For thirty years they fought and fought. Orsinium had walls made out of iron and, try as they might, they couldn't break through." "So what happened?" asked Baynarah. "You're so good at making up things that never happened, why don't you make it up?" So they did. Tay was the King of the Orcs, perched up in a tree they called Orsinium. Baynarah and Vaster played King Joile and Gaiden Shinji and they threw pebbles and sticks up at Tay while he taunted them in his most guttural voice. The three decided that the Goddess Kynareth (played by Baynarah in dual role) answered the prayers of Gaiden Shinji and drenched Orsinium in a torrent of rain. The walls rusted and dissolved. On cue, Tay obligingly fell from the tree and let King Joile and Gaiden Shinji mangle him with their enchanted blades. For the most of that summer, the year 675 of the First Era, Tay was nearly insensible by the power of the sun. There were no clouds, but it rained most every night, so the vegetation on the island of Gorne was bewildering lush. The stones themselves seemed to glow with sunlight, and the ditches burned with white meadowsweet and parsleydown; all around him were soft smells of flower and tree untroubled by wind; the foliage was purple green, blue green, ash green, white green. The wide cupolas, twisting cobbled streets, and thatched roofs of the little village of Gorne, and massive bleached rock of Sandil House all were magical to him. Yet the dreams haunted his nights and the Song continued whether he was awake or not. Against Aunt Ulliah's admonishments, Tay, Baynarah, and Vaster had breakfast outdoors every morning with the servants. Ulliah would hold an interior breakfast for herself and any visiting dignitaries: guests were rare, so she often ate alone. At first the servants would dine in silence, attempting gentility, but they broke down and would regale the children with gossip, reports, stories, and rumors. "Poor Arnyle is laid up with a fever again." "I'm telling you, they're cursed. The whole lot of 'em. Piss on the faerie and they piss right back on you." "Doesn't Little Miss Starsia look, oh, just a wee bit tight around the belly region late-ly?" "She's not!" The only servant who didn't speak at all was Tay's nursemaid Edebah. She wasn't pretty like the other maids, but the scars on her face did not deform her. Her poorly set broken nose and her short hair gave her a certain alien mystique. She would merely quietly smile at the gossip, and look at Tay with almost frightening love and devotion. One day, after breakfast, Baynarah whispered to Tay and Vaster, "We have to go to the hills on the other side of the island." She had used such imperatives before and always had something wonderful to show: a waterfall, tucked away behind ferns and tall rocks; a sunny grove of figs; a discreet still some peasants had set up; a sickly oak, twisted into a kneeling human figure; a collapsed stone wall that they imagined was thousands of years old, the last refuge of a doomed princess they named Merella. The three walked across through the forest until they came to a clearing. A few hundred feet beyond, the meadow sank to a dry creek bed, filled with small, smooth stones. They followed that into the dark woods where trees canopied high over their heads. Sporadic red and yellow blossoms burst along the moist underbrush, but they became rarer and rarer as the children marched on under the umbrageous oaks and elms. The air crackled with birds ticking a staccato choral piece, a minor chord of the Song. "Where are we going?" asked Tay. "It's not where we're going, it's what we're going to see," replied Baynarah. The forest surrounded the three children completely, bathed them in its tenebrous hues, and breathed on them with wet chirrups and sighs. It was easy for them to imagine that they were within a monster, walking along its twisted spine of stones. Baynarah scrambled up the steep hill and peered through the thick mass of shrub and tree. Tay lifted Vaster out of the creek bed and climbed out, gripping soft grass for support. There was no path through the forest here. Brambles and low hanging branches struck at them like the claws of chained beasts. The cries of the birds became ever more stentorious, as if angered at the invasion. One limb drew blood on Vaster's cheek, but he didn't cry out. Even Baynarah, who could pass like an ethereal creature through impenetrable forests, had a braid catch on a bramble, ruining the intricate pattern a servant had woven hours before. She paused to pull out the other braid, so her bright unruly tresses fell freely behind her. Now she was something wild, a nymph guiding the other two through her woodland domain. The Song began to beat like a wild pulse. They were on a shelf of stone below a cliff overlooking a tremendous gorge, staring over an expanse of cinder. It looked like the scene of a tremendous battle, a holocaust of fire. Charred boxes, weaponry, animal bones, and detritus too annihilated to be identifiable littered the ground. Speechless, Tay and Vaster stepped into the black field. Baynarah smiled, proud that she had finally found something of true wonder and mystery. "What is this place?" asked Vaster at last. "I don't know," Baynarah shrugged. "I thought at first that it was some kind of ruin, but now I think it's a junk pile, just not like any junk pile I've ever seen. Just look at this stuff." The three began an unorganized survey of the dusty mounds of refuse. Baynarah found a twisted sword only lightly blackened by flame and began polishing it to read the inscriptions on the blade. Vaster amused himself by breaking brittle boxes with his hands and feet, imagining himself a giant of unbelievable strength. A battered shield attracted Tay: there was something about it that reverberated with the sound of the Song. He pulled it out, and wiped its surface clean. "I've never seen that crest before," said Baynarah, looking over Tay's shoulder. "I think I have, but I don't remember," Tay whispered, trying to conjure the memory from his dreams. He was sure he had seen it there. "Look at this!" Vaster cried, interrupting Tay's thoughts. The boy was holding up a crystal orb. As his hand moved over the surface, brushing away grit and dust, a key in the Song rose which sent a shiver through Tay's entire body. Baynarah ran over to look at Vaster's treasure, but Tay felt paralyzed. "Where did you find that?" she gasped, gazing into the swirl beneath the crystal surface. "Over in that wagon," Vaster gestured toward a heap of blackened wood, barely discernible from the other piles but for its cart spokes. Baynarah began digging into the half-collapsed structure, so only her feet could be seen. The Song built in potency, sweeping over Tay. He began walking toward Vaster slowly. "Give me that," he whispered in a voice he could barely recognize as his own. "No," Vaster whispered back, his eyes locked on the colors reflected in the heart of the globe. "It's mine." Baynarah dug through the remains of the wagon for several more minutes, but she could find no treasures like Vaster's. Most everything within was destroyed, and what remained was common-place by any standards: broken arrows, armor shards, guar bones. Frustrated, she pulled herself out into the sunlight. Tay was alone, at the edge of the great gorge. "Where's Vaster?" Tay blinked and then turned back to his cousin with a shrug and a grin: "He went back to show everyone his new plunder. Did you find anything interesting?" "Not really," said Baynarah. "We probably ought to get back home before Vaster tells them anything that'll get us in trouble." Tay and Baynarah started the walk back at a quick pace. Tay knew that Vaster would not be there when they got back. He would never be returning home again. The crystal globe rested snugly in Tay's satchel, hidden under a pile of junk he had picked up. With all his heart, he prayed for the Song to return and drown out the memory of the gorge and the long, silent fall down. The boy had been so surprised, he hadn't even time to scream.

毒之歌 卷一 布里斯汀·泽尔 著

[pagebreak]

一切又开始了。尽管看似万籁俱寂（炉中沉火毕剥；年轻的女仆带着孩子在门边的椅子上沉睡；织了一半的毯子挂在墙上，等待着明日完成；窗外月亮中的一颗能够透过乳白色的云朵见着；孤零零的一只鸟藏在房椽后面安详地咕咕叫），忒伊听到那首歌的第一道和弦在遥远的某处突兀奏响。 房椽上的鸟儿呱呱地飞出窗外，女仆怀中的婴儿哭叫着惊醒。乐声渐强，曲调依然玄妙庄严。一切的运转似乎都依着音乐的旋律，仿佛一场怪诞的舞蹈正在上演：女仆在窗边站起，云朵被下方的炼狱染红，她的尖叫消解于歌声之中，因此寂然无声。之后发生的一切忒伊已经见过了太多次，真实地几乎不像是一场噩梦。 他完全不记得来到戈尼岛之前的事情，但他知道自己的过去必然有什么不同之处，将他和其他堂亲同辈区别开来。不只是因为他的父母双双亡故。他的堂姐贝娜拉的父母同样在那场战争中死去。也不是说戈尼岛上或者紧邻的哀伤之城里的家族成员排挤他。他们待他以礼貌的冷淡，就像所有因多瑞尔族人对待其他碍事的八岁男孩一样。 但是不知何故，忒伊无比肯定，自己孑然独立，与众不同。因为他总是听到一首歌，总是做那些噩梦。 “你单纯是想象力丰富。”乌莉雅姑姑会笑着耐心说道，然后摆摆手支开他，好重新忙自己的经文和家务。 “与众不同？世上所有人都觉得自己‘与众不同’，结果这个看法本身成为了一个普遍化观点。”他的堂兄卡寇斯如是说，他在为担任神殿祭司而学习，对悖论理解很深。 “如果在根本没有音乐的时候你还跟别人说你能听到音乐，他们会说你疯了，把你埋到谢尔格拉祭坛底下去。”他的叔叔特里菲斯会这样大吼，然后大步走开忙他自己的事。 只有他的保姆艾德芭会认真地听他讲述，并且带着淡淡的骄傲表情点头。但她除此以外不发一言。 他的堂姐兼首席玩伴贝娜拉对他说的歌声和噩梦最不感兴趣。 “忒伊，你老是讲这些真是烦。”贝娜拉说道，他、她和一个小堂弟瓦斯特走进花丛中的一块空地。草地很矮，刚及他们的脚踝，还有前年秋天留下的大堆黑色树叶。“现在，我们能不谈这个了吗？我们该玩什么呢？” 忒伊想了一会儿。“我们可以玩奥辛纽姆之围。” “那是什么？”瓦斯特问道，他是他们的常伴，比他们小三岁。 “奥辛纽姆是兽人的家园，就在沃斯嘉山脉。几百年来，它一直在扩大，越来越大，越来越大。兽人会从山上下来，劫掠整个高岩。然后，匕落的乔勒国王和蒂亚戈纳教团的盖登·辛吉以及其他来自哨卫城的人，我记不清了，都联合起来对抗奥辛纽姆。三十年，他们打了又打。奥辛纽姆的墙是用铁做的，尽管他们很努力，但还是无法攻入。” “后来呢？”贝娜拉问道。 “你这么会编故事，干嘛不编一下呢。” 于是他们就这样做了。忒伊是兽人的国王，坐在一棵他们称为奥辛纽姆的树上。贝娜拉和瓦斯特扮演乔勒国王和盖登·辛吉，他们向忒伊投掷石子和树枝，而忒伊则用他最阴沉的声音嘲弄他们。三人决定，让女神凯娜瑞丝（由贝娜拉分饰两角）回应了盖登·辛吉的祈祷，并将奥辛纽姆淹没在一场大雨中。城墙生锈溶解了。这时，忒伊顺从地从树上跳下来，让乔勒国王和盖登·辛吉用他们的附魔刀剑把他碎尸万段。 在第一纪元675年那个夏天的大部分时间里，忒伊几乎察觉不到太阳的威力。虽说万里无云，但每天晚上都会下雨，所以戈尼岛上的植被繁茂得眼花缭乱。石头本身似乎在阳光下发光，沟渠里燃烧着白色的绣线菊和欧芹；在他周围都是柔和的花香和树香，风儿吹拂也吹不消散；树叶是紫绿、蓝绿、灰绿和白绿。戈尼小村庄宽大的穹顶、曲折的鹅卵石街道和茅草屋顶，以及桑迪尔宅邸巨大的漂白岩石，通通令他感到惊奇。 然而那些梦仍然在夜里纠缠着他，此外无论是否清醒，那首歌始终缭绕不绝。 不顾乌莉雅姑姑的劝告，忒伊、贝娜拉和瓦斯特每天早上都在户外与仆人们一起吃早餐。乌莉雅会为自己和来访的贵宾准备室内早餐：客人不常来，所以她经常独自用餐。一开始仆人们会默默地吃饭，试图表现得彬彬有礼，但他们很快就会憋不住，向孩子们讲述八卦、消息、故事和传闻来逗他们开心。 “可怜的阿妮尔又发烧了。” “我告诉你，他们被诅咒了。他们所有人都是。在小仙子身上撒尿，他们就会在你身上撒尿。” “斯达莎小小姐看起来，哦，是不是肚子那里胀得有一点点紧啊？” “她才没有！” 仆人之中只有忒伊的保姆艾德芭一言不发，她不像其他女仆那样漂亮，但她脸上的疤痕并没有让脸走形。她那可怜的断鼻和短发给她带来了某种异样的神秘感。她只是静静地对那些闲言碎语微笑，并以近乎令人恐惧的爱和虔诚的目光看着忒伊。 有一天早餐后，贝娜拉悄悄对忒伊和瓦斯特说：“我们得去岛的另一边山上。” 她以前也这么发号施令过，而且总能让他们大开眼界：一个藏在蕨类植物和高耸岩石后面的瀑布；一片阳光明媚的无花果树林；一个农民们搭建的秘密蒸馏房；一棵病态的橡树，扭曲成一个跪着的人形；一堵坍塌的石墙，在他们想象中它的历史有数千年之久，是一个落难公主最后的避难所，他们叫她梅拉。 三个人穿过森林，直到他们来到一片林中空地。在几百尺之外，草地下沉到一条干涸小溪的河床，上面布满了光滑的小石头。他们沿着小溪进入黑暗的树林，那里的树木高高地笼罩在他们头上。零星的红色和黄色花朵在潮湿的灌木丛中绽放，但随着孩子们在巨大的橡树和榆树下行进，它们变得越来越稀少。空气中弥漫着鸟儿嘀嗒嘀嗒的合唱声，汇成那首歌的一个小和弦。 “我们要去哪里？”忒伊问。 “不是我们要去哪里，而是我们要去看什么。”贝娜拉回答。 森林完全包围了这三个孩子，让他们沐浴在它的阴暗色泽中，向他们呼出湿润的鸣叫与叹息。他们很容易就能想象自己是在一个怪物体内，沿着它扭曲的石头脊柱行走。 贝娜拉爬上陡峭的山坡，透过厚厚的灌木和树木窥看。忒伊将瓦斯特拉出小溪河床，抓着柔软的小草爬了出来。这里没有穿过森林的道路。荆棘和低垂的树枝像被锁住的野兽的爪子一样向他们袭来。鸟儿的叫声变得越来越激烈，似乎对入侵感到愤怒。一根树枝在瓦斯特的脸颊上划出了血痕，但他没有喊叫。即使是能够像飘渺幽灵一样穿过密不透风的森林的贝娜拉，也有一条辫子被荆棘扯住，弄乱了一个仆人几个小时前编织的繁复样式。她停下解开了另一条辫子，于是她明艳的一头乱发在她身后散开。现在她散发着野性，成了领着另外两人穿过她林地的宁芙。歌声开始像狂野的脉搏一样律动。 他们在一个悬崖下的突出石架上俯瞰着伟岸的峡谷，凝视着下面一大片灰烬。这看起来就像一场惨烈大战和烈焰留下的浩劫。烧焦的箱子、武器、兽骨，以及损毁得无法辨认的碎渣散落在地上。忒伊和瓦斯特哑口无言地踏上了黑色的地面。贝娜拉笑了，她为自己终于找到了真正奇异和神秘的事物而得意。 “这是什么地方？”最后瓦斯特问道。 “我不知道，”贝娜拉耸了耸肩。“我一开始以为这是什么废墟，但现在我认为这是一个垃圾堆，只是和我见过的其他垃圾堆都不太一样。看看这些东西就知道了。” 三人开始各自漫无目的地在这些尘土飞扬的垃圾堆中翻找。贝纳拉找到了一把被火焰微微烧黑的曲剑，开始擦拭它以阅读剑刃上的铭文。瓦斯特自娱自乐，手脚并用地打碎脆弱的箱子，想象自己是一个力大无比的巨人。一面破旧的盾牌吸引了泰勒：上面有什么东西激起了歌声回荡。他把它拉出来，把表面擦干净。 “我以前从未见过这个徽章。”贝娜拉从忒伊的肩膀上探过头来。 “我想我见过，但我不记得了。”泰勒低声说，试图唤起他梦中的记忆。他确信他在梦里看到过它。 “看看这个！”瓦斯特喊道，打断了忒伊的思绪。男孩举起了一个水晶球。当他的手在表面移动，拂去砂砾和灰尘时，歌声高了一阶，忒伊浑身颤抖。贝娜拉跑过去看瓦斯特的宝物，忒伊怔在原地。 “你在哪里找到的？”她喘着气，凝视着水晶表面下的漩涡。 “在那辆车上。”瓦斯特指向一堆发黑的木头，除了它的车辐外，几乎无法从其他一堆堆废物中将它辨认出来。贝娜拉开始在半塌的木架中翻找，只有她的脚露了出来。歌声的力量渐渐强大起来，席卷了忒伊全身。他开始慢慢走向瓦斯特。 “把它给我。”他用几乎认不出是自己的声音沉声说。 “不，”瓦斯特小声回道，他的目光定定望着在球体中心反射出来的色彩。“这是我的。” 贝娜拉在车骸中又挖了几分钟，但她没有找到像瓦斯特那样的宝物。里面的大部分东西都已经毁坏，剩下的东西怎么看都平平无奇：断箭、盔甲碎片、瓜尔骨头。她失望地抽出身子回到阳光底下。 忒伊独自一人站在大峡谷边上。 “瓦斯特去哪了？” 忒伊眨了眨眼，然后回过头来，耸了耸肩，笑着对他的堂姐说。“他回去向大家炫耀他的战利品了。你找到什么了吗？” “并没有，”贝娜拉说，“我们也许应该在瓦斯特说漏嘴给我们惹麻烦之前回家去。” 忒伊和贝娜拉开始快步往回走。忒伊知道他们回去时瓦斯特不会在那里。他永远回不了家了。水晶球紧紧地躺在忒伊的书包里，藏在他捡来的一堆杂物下面。他全心全意地祈求歌声复返，淹没峡谷中留下的记忆，淹没那漫长而沉默的坠落。那孩子太过震惊，他甚至来不及尖叫。

the poison song book i by bristin xel

t was beginning again. even though everything seemed serene (the last embers crackling in the hearth; young servant girl and her child slumbering in a chair by the door; a tapestry half-finished against the wall, waiting to be completed tomorrow; one of the moons visible through a milky cloud outside the window; a lone bird, out of sight in the rafters, cooing placidly), tay heard the first chords of the song strike dissonantly somewhere far away. the bird in the rafters croaked and took flight through the window. the baby in the girl's arms woke and began to scream. the song swelled in intensity, yet still remained subtle and stately in tempo. the movement of everything seemed to take on the rhythm of the music as if strange choreography had been staged: the girl rising to the window, the clouds reflecting back red from the inferno below, her scream, all muted, consumed by the song. everything that came thereafter tay had seen so many times, it had almost ceased to be a nightmare. he did not remember anything of his life before coming to the island of gorne, but he understood that there was something different in his past that set him apart from his cousins. it wasn't simply that his parents were dead. his cousin baynarah's parents had also died in the war. nor were the other housemen on gorne or nearby mournhold unusually cruel to him. they treated him with the same polite indifference that any indoril has for every other eight-year-old boy that got underfoot. but somehow, with absolutely certainty, tay knew he was alone. different. because of a song he always heard, and his nightmares. "you're certainly imaginative," his aunt ulliah would smile patiently, before waving him away so she could return to her scriptures and chores. "different? everyone in the world thinks they're 'different,' that's what makes it such a common sentiment," said his older cousin kalkorith who was studying to be temple priest and had a firm grasp on paradoxes. "if you tell anyone else that you keep hearing music where there's no music to be heard, they'll call you mad and bury you in the shrine of sheogorath," his uncle triffith would snarl, before striding away to attend his business. only his nursemaid edebah would listen to him seriously, and just nod with a faint look of pride. but she would never say another word. his cousin and chief playmate baynarah was by far the least interested in the stories of his song and his dreams. "how tiresome you are with all this, tay," said baynarah, after luncheon the summer of his eighth year. he, she, and a younger cousin vaster walked into a clearing in the midst of flowering trees. the grass was very low, barely up to their ankles, and there were big black piles of leaves from the previous autumn. "now, shall we get back to it? what shall we play?" tay thought for a moment. "we could play the siege of orsinium." "what's that?" asked vaster, their constant companion, three years their junior. "orsinium was the home of the orcs, off in the wrothgarian mountains. for hundreds of years, it kept growing bigger and bigger and bigger. the orcs would come down out of the mountains and rape and pillage all over high rock. and then, king joile of daggerfall and gaiden shinji of the order of diagna and someone else, i forget, from sentinel all joined together against orsinium. for thirty years they fought and fought. orsinium had walls made out of iron and, try as they might, they couldn't break through." "so what happened?" asked baynarah. "you're so good at making up things that never happened, why don't you make it up?" so they did. tay was the king of the orcs, perched up in a tree they called orsinium. baynarah and vaster played king joile and gaiden shinji and they threw pebbles and sticks up at tay while he taunted them in his most guttural voice. the three decided that the goddess kynareth (played by baynarah in dual role) answered the prayers of gaiden shinji and drenched orsinium in a torrent of rain. the walls rusted and dissolved. on cue, tay obligingly fell from the tree and let king joile and gaiden shinji mangle him with their enchanted blades. for the most of that summer, the year 675 of the first era, tay was nearly insensible by the power of the sun. there were no clouds, but it rained most every night, so the vegetation on the island of gorne was bewildering lush. the stones themselves seemed to glow with sunlight, and the ditches burned with white meadowsweet and parsleydown; all around him were soft smells of flower and tree untroubled by wind; the foliage was purple green, blue green, ash green, white green. the wide cupolas, twisting cobbled streets, and thatched roofs of the little village of gorne, and massive bleached rock of sandil house all were magical to him. yet the dreams haunted his nights and the song continued whether he was awake or not. against aunt ulliah's admonishments, tay, baynarah, and vaster had breakfast outdoors every morning with the servants. ulliah would hold an interior breakfast for herself and any visiting dignitaries: guests were rare, so she often ate alone. at first the servants would dine in silence, attempting gentility, but they broke down and would regale the children with gossip, reports, stories, and rumors. "poor arnyle is laid up with a fever again." "i'm telling you, they're cursed. the whole lot of 'em. piss on the faerie and they piss right back on you." "doesn't little miss starsia look, oh, just a wee bit tight around the belly region late-ly?" "she's not!" the only servant who didn't speak at all was tay's nursemaid edebah. she wasn't pretty like the other maids, but the scars on her face did not deform her. her poorly set broken nose and her short hair gave her a certain alien mystique. she would merely quietly smile at the gossip, and look at tay with almost frightening love and devotion. one day, after breakfast, baynarah whispered to tay and vaster, "we have to go to the hills on the other side of the island." she had used such imperatives before and always had something wonderful to show: a waterfall, tucked away behind ferns and tall rocks; a sunny grove of figs; a discreet still some peasants had set up; a sickly oak, twisted into a kneeling human figure; a collapsed stone wall that they imagined was thousands of years old, the last refuge of a doomed princess they named merella. the three walked across through the forest until they came to a clearing. a few hundred feet beyond, the meadow sank to a dry creek bed, filled with small, smooth stones. they followed that into the dark woods where trees canopied high over their heads. sporadic red and yellow blossoms burst along the moist underbrush, but they became rarer and rarer as the children marched on under the umbrageous oaks and elms. the air crackled with birds ticking a staccato choral piece, a minor chord of the song. "where are we going?" asked tay. "it's not where we're going, it's what we're going to see," replied baynarah. the forest surrounded the three children completely, bathed them in its tenebrous hues, and breathed on them with wet chirrups and sighs. it was easy for them to imagine that they were within a monster, walking along its twisted spine of stones. baynarah scrambled up the steep hill and peered through the thick mass of shrub and tree. tay lifted vaster out of the creek bed and climbed out, gripping soft grass for support. there was no path through the forest here. brambles and low hanging branches struck at them like the claws of chained beasts. the cries of the birds became ever more stentorious, as if angered at the invasion. one limb drew blood on vaster's cheek, but he didn't cry out. even baynarah, who could pass like an ethereal creature through impenetrable forests, had a braid catch on a bramble, ruining the intricate pattern a servant had woven hours before. she paused to pull out the other braid, so her bright unruly tresses fell freely behind her. now she was something wild, a nymph guiding the other two through her woodland domain. the song began to beat like a wild pulse. they were on a shelf of stone below a cliff overlooking a tremendous gorge, staring over an expanse of cinder. it looked like the scene of a tremendous battle, a holocaust of fire. charred boxes, weaponry, animal bones, and detritus too annihilated to be identifiable littered the ground. speechless, tay and vaster stepped into the black field. baynarah smiled, proud that she had finally found something of true wonder and mystery. "what is this place?" asked vaster at last. "i don't know," baynarah shrugged. "i thought at first that it was some kind of ruin, but now i think it's a junk pile, just not like any junk pile i've ever seen. just look at this stuff." the three began an unorganized survey of the dusty mounds of refuse. baynarah found a twisted sword only lightly blackened by flame and began polishing it to read the inscriptions on the blade. vaster amused himself by breaking brittle boxes with his hands and feet, imagining himself a giant of unbelievable strength. a battered shield attracted tay: there was something about it that reverberated with the sound of the song. he pulled it out, and wiped its surface clean. "i've never seen that crest before," said baynarah, looking over tay's shoulder. "i think i have, but i don't remember," tay whispered, trying to conjure the memory from his dreams. he was sure he had seen it there. "look at this!" vaster cried, interrupting tay's thoughts. the boy was holding up a crystal orb. as his hand moved over the surface, brushing away grit and dust, a key in the song rose which sent a shiver through tay's entire body. baynarah ran over to look at vaster's treasure, but tay felt paralyzed. "where did you find that?" she gasped, gazing into the swirl beneath the crystal surface. "over in that wagon," vaster gestured toward a heap of blackened wood, barely discernible from the other piles but for its cart spokes. baynarah began digging into the half-collapsed structure, so only her feet could be seen. the song built in potency, sweeping over tay. he began walking toward vaster slowly. "give me that," he whispered in a voice he could barely recognize as his own. "no," vaster whispered back, his eyes locked on the colors reflected in the heart of the globe. "it's mine." baynarah dug through the remains of the wagon for several more minutes, but she could find no treasures like vaster's. most everything within was destroyed, and what remained was common-place by any standards: broken arrows, armor shards, guar bones. frustrated, she pulled herself out into the sunlight. tay was alone, at the edge of the great gorge. "where's vaster?" tay blinked and then turned back to his cousin with a shrug and a grin: "he went back to show everyone his new plunder. did you find anything interesting?" "not really," said baynarah. "we probably ought to get back home before vaster tells them anything that'll get us in trouble." tay and baynarah started the walk back at a quick pace. tay knew that vaster would not be there when they got back. he would never be returning home again. the crystal globe rested snugly in tay's satchel, hidden under a pile of junk he had picked up. with all his heart, he prayed for the song to return and drown out the memory of the gorge and the long, silent fall down. the boy had been so surprised, he hadn't even time to scream.

[pagebreak]

毒之歌 卷一 布里斯汀·泽尔 著

[pagebreak]

一切又开始了。尽管看似万籁俱寂（炉中沉火毕剥；年轻的女仆带着孩子在门边的椅子上沉睡；织了一半的毯子挂在墙上，等待着明日完成；窗外月亮中的一颗能够透过乳白色的云朵见着；孤零零的一只鸟藏在房椽后面安详地咕咕叫），忒伊听到那首歌的第一道和弦在遥远的某处突兀奏响。 房椽上的鸟儿呱呱地飞出窗外，女仆怀中的婴儿哭叫着惊醒。乐声渐强，曲调依然玄妙庄严。一切的运转似乎都依着音乐的旋律，仿佛一场怪诞的舞蹈正在上演：女仆在窗边站起，云朵被下方的炼狱染红，她的尖叫消解于歌声之中，因此寂然无声。之后发生的一切忒伊已经见过了太多次，真实地几乎不像是一场噩梦。 他完全不记得来到戈尼岛之前的事情，但他知道自己的过去必然有什么不同之处，将他和其他堂亲同辈区别开来。不只是因为他的父母双双亡故。他的堂姐贝娜拉的父母同样在那场战争中死去。也不是说戈尼岛上或者紧邻的哀伤之城里的家族成员排挤他。他们待他以礼貌的冷淡，就像所有因多瑞尔族人对待其他碍事的八岁男孩一样。 但是不知何故，忒伊无比肯定，自己孑然独立，与众不同。因为他总是听到一首歌，总是做那些噩梦。 “你单纯是想象力丰富。”乌莉雅姑姑会笑着耐心说道，然后摆摆手支开他，好重新忙自己的经文和家务。 “与众不同？世上所有人都觉得自己‘与众不同’，结果这个看法本身成为了一个普遍化观点。”他的堂兄卡寇斯如是说，他在为担任神殿祭司而学习，对悖论理解很深。 “如果在根本没有音乐的时候你还跟别人说你能听到音乐，他们会说你疯了，把你埋到谢尔格拉祭坛底下去。”他的叔叔特里菲斯会这样大吼，然后大步走开忙他自己的事。 只有他的保姆艾德芭会认真地听他讲述，并且带着淡淡的骄傲表情点头。但她除此以外不发一言。 他的堂姐兼首席玩伴贝娜拉对他说的歌声和噩梦最不感兴趣。 “忒伊，你老是讲这些真是烦。”贝娜拉说道，他、她和一个小堂弟瓦斯特走进花丛中的一块空地。草地很矮，刚及他们的脚踝，还有前年秋天留下的大堆黑色树叶。“现在，我们能不谈这个了吗？我们该玩什么呢？” 忒伊想了一会儿。“我们可以玩奥辛纽姆之围。” “那是什么？”瓦斯特问道，他是他们的常伴，比他们小三岁。 “奥辛纽姆是兽人的家园，就在沃斯嘉山脉。几百年来，它一直在扩大，越来越大，越来越大。兽人会从山上下来，劫掠整个高岩。然后，匕落的乔勒国王和蒂亚戈纳教团的盖登·辛吉以及其他来自哨卫城的人，我记不清了，都联合起来对抗奥辛纽姆。三十年，他们打了又打。奥辛纽姆的墙是用铁做的，尽管他们很努力，但还是无法攻入。” “后来呢？”贝娜拉问道。 “你这么会编故事，干嘛不编一下呢。” 于是他们就这样做了。忒伊是兽人的国王，坐在一棵他们称为奥辛纽姆的树上。贝娜拉和瓦斯特扮演乔勒国王和盖登·辛吉，他们向忒伊投掷石子和树枝，而忒伊则用他最阴沉的声音嘲弄他们。三人决定，让女神凯娜瑞丝（由贝娜拉分饰两角）回应了盖登·辛吉的祈祷，并将奥辛纽姆淹没在一场大雨中。城墙生锈溶解了。这时，忒伊顺从地从树上跳下来，让乔勒国王和盖登·辛吉用他们的附魔刀剑把他碎尸万段。 在第一纪元675年那个夏天的大部分时间里，忒伊几乎察觉不到太阳的威力。虽说万里无云，但每天晚上都会下雨，所以戈尼岛上的植被繁茂得眼花缭乱。石头本身似乎在阳光下发光，沟渠里燃烧着白色的绣线菊和欧芹；在他周围都是柔和的花香和树香，风儿吹拂也吹不消散；树叶是紫绿、蓝绿、灰绿和白绿。戈尼小村庄宽大的穹顶、曲折的鹅卵石街道和茅草屋顶，以及桑迪尔宅邸巨大的漂白岩石，通通令他感到惊奇。 然而那些梦仍然在夜里纠缠着他，此外无论是否清醒，那首歌始终缭绕不绝。 不顾乌莉雅姑姑的劝告，忒伊、贝娜拉和瓦斯特每天早上都在户外与仆人们一起吃早餐。乌莉雅会为自己和来访的贵宾准备室内早餐：客人不常来，所以她经常独自用餐。一开始仆人们会默默地吃饭，试图表现得彬彬有礼，但他们很快就会憋不住，向孩子们讲述八卦、消息、故事和传闻来逗他们开心。 “可怜的阿妮尔又发烧了。” “我告诉你，他们被诅咒了。他们所有人都是。在小仙子身上撒尿，他们就会在你身上撒尿。” “斯达莎小小姐看起来，哦，是不是肚子那里胀得有一点点紧啊？” “她才没有！” 仆人之中只有忒伊的保姆艾德芭一言不发，她不像其他女仆那样漂亮，但她脸上的疤痕并没有让脸走形。她那可怜的断鼻和短发给她带来了某种异样的神秘感。她只是静静地对那些闲言碎语微笑，并以近乎令人恐惧的爱和虔诚的目光看着忒伊。 有一天早餐后，贝娜拉悄悄对忒伊和瓦斯特说：“我们得去岛的另一边山上。” 她以前也这么发号施令过，而且总能让他们大开眼界：一个藏在蕨类植物和高耸岩石后面的瀑布；一片阳光明媚的无花果树林；一个农民们搭建的秘密蒸馏房；一棵病态的橡树，扭曲成一个跪着的人形；一堵坍塌的石墙，在他们想象中它的历史有数千年之久，是一个落难公主最后的避难所，他们叫她梅拉。 三个人穿过森林，直到他们来到一片林中空地。在几百尺之外，草地下沉到一条干涸小溪的河床，上面布满了光滑的小石头。他们沿着小溪进入黑暗的树林，那里的树木高高地笼罩在他们头上。零星的红色和黄色花朵在潮湿的灌木丛中绽放，但随着孩子们在巨大的橡树和榆树下行进，它们变得越来越稀少。空气中弥漫着鸟儿嘀嗒嘀嗒的合唱声，汇成那首歌的一个小和弦。 “我们要去哪里？”忒伊问。 “不是我们要去哪里，而是我们要去看什么。”贝娜拉回答。 森林完全包围了这三个孩子，让他们沐浴在它的阴暗色泽中，向他们呼出湿润的鸣叫与叹息。他们很容易就能想象自己是在一个怪物体内，沿着它扭曲的石头脊柱行走。 贝娜拉爬上陡峭的山坡，透过厚厚的灌木和树木窥看。忒伊将瓦斯特拉出小溪河床，抓着柔软的小草爬了出来。这里没有穿过森林的道路。荆棘和低垂的树枝像被锁住的野兽的爪子一样向他们袭来。鸟儿的叫声变得越来越激烈，似乎对入侵感到愤怒。一根树枝在瓦斯特的脸颊上划出了血痕，但他没有喊叫。即使是能够像飘渺幽灵一样穿过密不透风的森林的贝娜拉，也有一条辫子被荆棘扯住，弄乱了一个仆人几个小时前编织的繁复样式。她停下解开了另一条辫子，于是她明艳的一头乱发在她身后散开。现在她散发着野性，成了领着另外两人穿过她林地的宁芙。歌声开始像狂野的脉搏一样律动。 他们在一个悬崖下的突出石架上俯瞰着伟岸的峡谷，凝视着下面一大片灰烬。这看起来就像一场惨烈大战和烈焰留下的浩劫。烧焦的箱子、武器、兽骨，以及损毁得无法辨认的碎渣散落在地上。忒伊和瓦斯特哑口无言地踏上了黑色的地面。贝娜拉笑了，她为自己终于找到了真正奇异和神秘的事物而得意。 “这是什么地方？”最后瓦斯特问道。 “我不知道，”贝娜拉耸了耸肩。“我一开始以为这是什么废墟，但现在我认为这是一个垃圾堆，只是和我见过的其他垃圾堆都不太一样。看看这些东西就知道了。” 三人开始各自漫无目的地在这些尘土飞扬的垃圾堆中翻找。贝纳拉找到了一把被火焰微微烧黑的曲剑，开始擦拭它以阅读剑刃上的铭文。瓦斯特自娱自乐，手脚并用地打碎脆弱的箱子，想象自己是一个力大无比的巨人。一面破旧的盾牌吸引了泰勒：上面有什么东西激起了歌声回荡。他把它拉出来，把表面擦干净。 “我以前从未见过这个徽章。”贝娜拉从忒伊的肩膀上探过头来。 “我想我见过，但我不记得了。”泰勒低声说，试图唤起他梦中的记忆。他确信他在梦里看到过它。 “看看这个！”瓦斯特喊道，打断了忒伊的思绪。男孩举起了一个水晶球。当他的手在表面移动，拂去砂砾和灰尘时，歌声高了一阶，忒伊浑身颤抖。贝娜拉跑过去看瓦斯特的宝物，忒伊怔在原地。 “你在哪里找到的？”她喘着气，凝视着水晶表面下的漩涡。 “在那辆车上。”瓦斯特指向一堆发黑的木头，除了它的车辐外，几乎无法从其他一堆堆废物中将它辨认出来。贝娜拉开始在半塌的木架中翻找，只有她的脚露了出来。歌声的力量渐渐强大起来，席卷了忒伊全身。他开始慢慢走向瓦斯特。 “把它给我。”他用几乎认不出是自己的声音沉声说。 “不，”瓦斯特小声回道，他的目光定定望着在球体中心反射出来的色彩。“这是我的。” 贝娜拉在车骸中又挖了几分钟，但她没有找到像瓦斯特那样的宝物。里面的大部分东西都已经毁坏，剩下的东西怎么看都平平无奇：断箭、盔甲碎片、瓜尔骨头。她失望地抽出身子回到阳光底下。 忒伊独自一人站在大峡谷边上。 “瓦斯特去哪了？” 忒伊眨了眨眼，然后回过头来，耸了耸肩，笑着对他的堂姐说。“他回去向大家炫耀他的战利品了。你找到什么了吗？” “并没有，”贝娜拉说，“我们也许应该在瓦斯特说漏嘴给我们惹麻烦之前回家去。” 忒伊和贝娜拉开始快步往回走。忒伊知道他们回去时瓦斯特不会在那里。他永远回不了家了。水晶球紧紧地躺在忒伊的书包里，藏在他捡来的一堆杂物下面。他全心全意地祈求歌声复返，淹没峡谷中留下的记忆，淹没那漫长而沉默的坠落。那孩子太过震惊，他甚至来不及尖叫。

t was beginning again. Even though everything seemed serene (the last embers crackling in the hearth; young servant girl and her child slumbering in a chair by the door; a tapestry half-finished against the wall, waiting to be completed tomorrow; one of the moons visible through a milky cloud outside the window; a lone bird, out of sight in the rafters, cooing placidly), Tay heard the first chords of the Song strike dissonantly somewhere far away. The bird in the rafters croaked and took flight through the window. The baby in the girl's arms woke and began to scream. The Song swelled in intensity, yet still remained subtle and stately in tempo. The movement of everything seemed to take on the rhythm of the music as if strange choreography had been staged: the girl rising to the window, the clouds reflecting back red from the inferno below, her scream, all muted, consumed by the Song. Everything that came thereafter Tay had seen so many times, it had almost ceased to be a nightmare. He did not remember anything of his life before coming to the island of Gorne, but he understood that there was something different in his past that set him apart from his cousins. It wasn't simply that his parents were dead. His cousin Baynarah's parents had also died in the War. Nor were the other Housemen on Gorne or nearby Mournhold unusually cruel to him. They treated him with the same polite indifference that any Indoril has for every other eight-year-old boy that got underfoot. But somehow, with absolutely certainty, Tay knew he was alone. Different. Because of a Song he always heard, and his nightmares. "You're certainly imaginative," his aunt Ulliah would smile patiently, before waving him away so she could return to her scriptures and chores. "Different? Everyone in the world thinks they're 'different,' that's what makes it such a common sentiment," said his older cousin Kalkorith who was studying to be Temple priest and had a firm grasp on paradoxes. "If you tell anyone else that you keep hearing music where there's no music to be heard, they'll call you mad and bury you in the Shrine of Sheogorath," his uncle Triffith would snarl, before striding away to attend his business. Only his nursemaid Edebah would listen to him seriously, and just nod with a faint look of pride. But she would never say another word. His cousin and chief playmate Baynarah was by far the least interested in the stories of his Song and his dreams. "How tiresome you are with all this, Tay," said Baynarah, after luncheon the summer of his eighth year. He, she, and a younger cousin Vaster walked into a clearing in the midst of flowering trees. The grass was very low, barely up to their ankles, and there were big black piles of leaves from the previous autumn. "Now, shall we get back to it? What shall we play?" Tay thought for a moment. "We could play the Siege of Orsinium." "What's that?" asked Vaster, their constant companion, three years their junior. "Orsinium was the home of the orcs, off in the Wrothgarian Mountains. For hundreds of years, it kept growing bigger and bigger and bigger. The orcs would come down out of the mountains and rape and pillage all over High Rock. And then, King Joile of Daggerfall and Gaiden Shinji of the Order of Diagna and someone else, I forget, from Sentinel all joined together against Orsinium. For thirty years they fought and fought. Orsinium had walls made out of iron and, try as they might, they couldn't break through." "So what happened?" asked Baynarah. "You're so good at making up things that never happened, why don't you make it up?" So they did. Tay was the King of the Orcs, perched up in a tree they called Orsinium. Baynarah and Vaster played King Joile and Gaiden Shinji and they threw pebbles and sticks up at Tay while he taunted them in his most guttural voice. The three decided that the Goddess Kynareth (played by Baynarah in dual role) answered the prayers of Gaiden Shinji and drenched Orsinium in a torrent of rain. The walls rusted and dissolved. On cue, Tay obligingly fell from the tree and let King Joile and Gaiden Shinji mangle him with their enchanted blades. For the most of that summer, the year 675 of the First Era, Tay was nearly insensible by the power of the sun. There were no clouds, but it rained most every night, so the vegetation on the island of Gorne was bewildering lush. The stones themselves seemed to glow with sunlight, and the ditches burned with white meadowsweet and parsleydown; all around him were soft smells of flower and tree untroubled by wind; the foliage was purple green, blue green, ash green, white green. The wide cupolas, twisting cobbled streets, and thatched roofs of the little village of Gorne, and massive bleached rock of Sandil House all were magical to him. Yet the dreams haunted his nights and the Song continued whether he was awake or not. Against Aunt Ulliah's admonishments, Tay, Baynarah, and Vaster had breakfast outdoors every morning with the servants. Ulliah would hold an interior breakfast for herself and any visiting dignitaries: guests were rare, so she often ate alone. At first the servants would dine in silence, attempting gentility, but they broke down and would regale the children with gossip, reports, stories, and rumors. "Poor Arnyle is laid up with a fever again." "I'm telling you, they're cursed. The whole lot of 'em. Piss on the faerie and they piss right back on you." "Doesn't Little Miss Starsia look, oh, just a wee bit tight around the belly region late-ly?" "She's not!" The only servant who didn't speak at all was Tay's nursemaid Edebah. She wasn't pretty like the other maids, but the scars on her face did not deform her. Her poorly set broken nose and her short hair gave her a certain alien mystique. She would merely quietly smile at the gossip, and look at Tay with almost frightening love and devotion. One day, after breakfast, Baynarah whispered to Tay and Vaster, "We have to go to the hills on the other side of the island." She had used such imperatives before and always had something wonderful to show: a waterfall, tucked away behind ferns and tall rocks; a sunny grove of figs; a discreet still some peasants had set up; a sickly oak, twisted into a kneeling human figure; a collapsed stone wall that they imagined was thousands of years old, the last refuge of a doomed princess they named Merella. The three walked across through the forest until they came to a clearing. A few hundred feet beyond, the meadow sank to a dry creek bed, filled with small, smooth stones. They followed that into the dark woods where trees canopied high over their heads. Sporadic red and yellow blossoms burst along the moist underbrush, but they became rarer and rarer as the children marched on under the umbrageous oaks and elms. The air crackled with birds ticking a staccato choral piece, a minor chord of the Song. "Where are we going?" asked Tay. "It's not where we're going, it's what we're going to see," replied Baynarah. The forest surrounded the three children completely, bathed them in its tenebrous hues, and breathed on them with wet chirrups and sighs. It was easy for them to imagine that they were within a monster, walking along its twisted spine of stones. Baynarah scrambled up the steep hill and peered through the thick mass of shrub and tree. Tay lifted Vaster out of the creek bed and climbed out, gripping soft grass for support. There was no path through the forest here. Brambles and low hanging branches struck at them like the claws of chained beasts. The cries of the birds became ever more stentorious, as if angered at the invasion. One limb drew blood on Vaster's cheek, but he didn't cry out. Even Baynarah, who could pass like an ethereal creature through impenetrable forests, had a braid catch on a bramble, ruining the intricate pattern a servant had woven hours before. She paused to pull out the other braid, so her bright unruly tresses fell freely behind her. Now she was something wild, a nymph guiding the other two through her woodland domain. The Song began to beat like a wild pulse. They were on a shelf of stone below a cliff overlooking a tremendous gorge, staring over an expanse of cinder. It looked like the scene of a tremendous battle, a holocaust of fire. Charred boxes, weaponry, animal bones, and detritus too annihilated to be identifiable littered the ground. Speechless, Tay and Vaster stepped into the black field. Baynarah smiled, proud that she had finally found something of true wonder and mystery. "What is this place?" asked Vaster at last. "I don't know," Baynarah shrugged. "I thought at first that it was some kind of ruin, but now I think it's a junk pile, just not like any junk pile I've ever seen. Just look at this stuff." The three began an unorganized survey of the dusty mounds of refuse. Baynarah found a twisted sword only lightly blackened by flame and began polishing it to read the inscriptions on the blade. Vaster amused himself by breaking brittle boxes with his hands and feet, imagining himself a giant of unbelievable strength. A battered shield attracted Tay: there was something about it that reverberated with the sound of the Song. He pulled it out, and wiped its surface clean. "I've never seen that crest before," said Baynarah, looking over Tay's shoulder. "I think I have, but I don't remember," Tay whispered, trying to conjure the memory from his dreams. He was sure he had seen it there. "Look at this!" Vaster cried, interrupting Tay's thoughts. The boy was holding up a crystal orb. As his hand moved over the surface, brushing away grit and dust, a key in the Song rose which sent a shiver through Tay's entire body. Baynarah ran over to look at Vaster's treasure, but Tay felt paralyzed. "Where did you find that?" she gasped, gazing into the swirl beneath the crystal surface. "Over in that wagon," Vaster gestured toward a heap of blackened wood, barely discernible from the other piles but for its cart spokes. Baynarah began digging into the half-collapsed structure, so only her feet could be seen. The Song built in potency, sweeping over Tay. He began walking toward Vaster slowly. "Give me that," he whispered in a voice he could barely recognize as his own. "No," Vaster whispered back, his eyes locked on the colors reflected in the heart of the globe. "It's mine." Baynarah dug through the remains of the wagon for several more minutes, but she could find no treasures like Vaster's. Most everything within was destroyed, and what remained was common-place by any standards: broken arrows, armor shards, guar bones. Frustrated, she pulled herself out into the sunlight. Tay was alone, at the edge of the great gorge. "Where's Vaster?" Tay blinked and then turned back to his cousin with a shrug and a grin: "He went back to show everyone his new plunder. Did you find anything interesting?" "Not really," said Baynarah. "We probably ought to get back home before Vaster tells them anything that'll get us in trouble." Tay and Baynarah started the walk back at a quick pace. Tay knew that Vaster would not be there when they got back. He would never be returning home again. The crystal globe rested snugly in Tay's satchel, hidden under a pile of junk he had picked up. With all his heart, he prayed for the Song to return and drown out the memory of the gorge and the long, silent fall down. The boy had been so surprised, he hadn't even time to scream. Chinese: [pagebreak]

毒之歌 卷一 布里斯汀·泽尔 著

[pagebreak]

一切又开始了。尽管看似万籁俱寂（炉中沉火毕剥；年轻的女仆带着孩子在门边的椅子上沉睡；织了一半的毯子挂在墙上，等待着明日完成；窗外月亮中的一颗能够透过乳白色的云朵见着；孤零零的一只鸟藏在房椽后面安详地咕咕叫），忒伊听到那首歌的第一道和弦在遥远的某处突兀奏响。 房椽上的鸟儿呱呱地飞出窗外，女仆怀中的婴儿哭叫着惊醒。乐声渐强，曲调依然玄妙庄严。一切的运转似乎都依着音乐的旋律，仿佛一场怪诞的舞蹈正在上演：女仆在窗边站起，云朵被下方的炼狱染红，她的尖叫消解于歌声之中，因此寂然无声。之后发生的一切忒伊已经见过了太多次，真实地几乎不像是一场噩梦。 他完全不记得来到戈尼岛之前的事情，但他知道自己的过去必然有什么不同之处，将他和其他堂亲同辈区别开来。不只是因为他的父母双双亡故。他的堂姐贝娜拉的父母同样在那场战争中死去。也不是说戈尼岛上或者紧邻的哀伤之城里的家族成员排挤他。他们待他以礼貌的冷淡，就像所有因多瑞尔族人对待其他碍事的八岁男孩一样。 但是不知何故，忒伊无比肯定，自己孑然独立，与众不同。因为他总是听到一首歌，总是做那些噩梦。 “你单纯是想象力丰富。”乌莉雅姑姑会笑着耐心说道，然后摆摆手支开他，好重新忙自己的经文和家务。 “与众不同？世上所有人都觉得自己‘与众不同’，结果这个看法本身成为了一个普遍化观点。”他的堂兄卡寇斯如是说，他在为担任神殿祭司而学习，对悖论理解很深。 “如果在根本没有音乐的时候你还跟别人说你能听到音乐，他们会说你疯了，把你埋到谢尔格拉祭坛底下去。”他的叔叔特里菲斯会这样大吼，然后大步走开忙他自己的事。 只有他的保姆艾德芭会认真地听他讲述，并且带着淡淡的骄傲表情点头。但她除此以外不发一言。 他的堂姐兼首席玩伴贝娜拉对他说的歌声和噩梦最不感兴趣。 “忒伊，你老是讲这些真是烦。”贝娜拉说道，他、她和一个小堂弟瓦斯特走进花丛中的一块空地。草地很矮，刚及他们的脚踝，还有前年秋天留下的大堆黑色树叶。“现在，我们能不谈这个了吗？我们该玩什么呢？” 忒伊想了一会儿。“我们可以玩奥辛纽姆之围。” “那是什么？”瓦斯特问道，他是他们的常伴，比他们小三岁。 “奥辛纽姆是兽人的家园，就在沃斯嘉山脉。几百年来，它一直在扩大，越来越大，越来越大。兽人会从山上下来，劫掠整个高岩。然后，匕落的乔勒国王和蒂亚戈纳教团的盖登·辛吉以及其他来自哨卫城的人，我记不清了，都联合起来对抗奥辛纽姆。三十年，他们打了又打。奥辛纽姆的墙是用铁做的，尽管他们很努力，但还是无法攻入。” “后来呢？”贝娜拉问道。 “你这么会编故事，干嘛不编一下呢。” 于是他们就这样做了。忒伊是兽人的国王，坐在一棵他们称为奥辛纽姆的树上。贝娜拉和瓦斯特扮演乔勒国王和盖登·辛吉，他们向忒伊投掷石子和树枝，而忒伊则用他最阴沉的声音嘲弄他们。三人决定，让女神凯娜瑞丝（由贝娜拉分饰两角）回应了盖登·辛吉的祈祷，并将奥辛纽姆淹没在一场大雨中。城墙生锈溶解了。这时，忒伊顺从地从树上跳下来，让乔勒国王和盖登·辛吉用他们的附魔刀剑把他碎尸万段。 在第一纪元675年那个夏天的大部分时间里，忒伊几乎察觉不到太阳的威力。虽说万里无云，但每天晚上都会下雨，所以戈尼岛上的植被繁茂得眼花缭乱。石头本身似乎在阳光下发光，沟渠里燃烧着白色的绣线菊和欧芹；在他周围都是柔和的花香和树香，风儿吹拂也吹不消散；树叶是紫绿、蓝绿、灰绿和白绿。戈尼小村庄宽大的穹顶、曲折的鹅卵石街道和茅草屋顶，以及桑迪尔宅邸巨大的漂白岩石，通通令他感到惊奇。 然而那些梦仍然在夜里纠缠着他，此外无论是否清醒，那首歌始终缭绕不绝。 不顾乌莉雅姑姑的劝告，忒伊、贝娜拉和瓦斯特每天早上都在户外与仆人们一起吃早餐。乌莉雅会为自己和来访的贵宾准备室内早餐：客人不常来，所以她经常独自用餐。一开始仆人们会默默地吃饭，试图表现得彬彬有礼，但他们很快就会憋不住，向孩子们讲述八卦、消息、故事和传闻来逗他们开心。 “可怜的阿妮尔又发烧了。” “我告诉你，他们被诅咒了。他们所有人都是。在小仙子身上撒尿，他们就会在你身上撒尿。” “斯达莎小小姐看起来，哦，是不是肚子那里胀得有一点点紧啊？” “她才没有！” 仆人之中只有忒伊的保姆艾德芭一言不发，她不像其他女仆那样漂亮，但她脸上的疤痕并没有让脸走形。她那可怜的断鼻和短发给她带来了某种异样的神秘感。她只是静静地对那些闲言碎语微笑，并以近乎令人恐惧的爱和虔诚的目光看着忒伊。 有一天早餐后，贝娜拉悄悄对忒伊和瓦斯特说：“我们得去岛的另一边山上。” 她以前也这么发号施令过，而且总能让他们大开眼界：一个藏在蕨类植物和高耸岩石后面的瀑布；一片阳光明媚的无花果树林；一个农民们搭建的秘密蒸馏房；一棵病态的橡树，扭曲成一个跪着的人形；一堵坍塌的石墙，在他们想象中它的历史有数千年之久，是一个落难公主最后的避难所，他们叫她梅拉。 三个人穿过森林，直到他们来到一片林中空地。在几百尺之外，草地下沉到一条干涸小溪的河床，上面布满了光滑的小石头。他们沿着小溪进入黑暗的树林，那里的树木高高地笼罩在他们头上。零星的红色和黄色花朵在潮湿的灌木丛中绽放，但随着孩子们在巨大的橡树和榆树下行进，它们变得越来越稀少。空气中弥漫着鸟儿嘀嗒嘀嗒的合唱声，汇成那首歌的一个小和弦。 “我们要去哪里？”忒伊问。 “不是我们要去哪里，而是我们要去看什么。”贝娜拉回答。 森林完全包围了这三个孩子，让他们沐浴在它的阴暗色泽中，向他们呼出湿润的鸣叫与叹息。他们很容易就能想象自己是在一个怪物体内，沿着它扭曲的石头脊柱行走。 贝娜拉爬上陡峭的山坡，透过厚厚的灌木和树木窥看。忒伊将瓦斯特拉出小溪河床，抓着柔软的小草爬了出来。这里没有穿过森林的道路。荆棘和低垂的树枝像被锁住的野兽的爪子一样向他们袭来。鸟儿的叫声变得越来越激烈，似乎对入侵感到愤怒。一根树枝在瓦斯特的脸颊上划出了血痕，但他没有喊叫。即使是能够像飘渺幽灵一样穿过密不透风的森林的贝娜拉，也有一条辫子被荆棘扯住，弄乱了一个仆人几个小时前编织的繁复样式。她停下解开了另一条辫子，于是她明艳的一头乱发在她身后散开。现在她散发着野性，成了领着另外两人穿过她林地的宁芙。歌声开始像狂野的脉搏一样律动。 他们在一个悬崖下的突出石架上俯瞰着伟岸的峡谷，凝视着下面一大片灰烬。这看起来就像一场惨烈大战和烈焰留下的浩劫。烧焦的箱子、武器、兽骨，以及损毁得无法辨认的碎渣散落在地上。忒伊和瓦斯特哑口无言地踏上了黑色的地面。贝娜拉笑了，她为自己终于找到了真正奇异和神秘的事物而得意。 “这是什么地方？”最后瓦斯特问道。 “我不知道，”贝娜拉耸了耸肩。“我一开始以为这是什么废墟，但现在我认为这是一个垃圾堆，只是和我见过的其他垃圾堆都不太一样。看看这些东西就知道了。” 三人开始各自漫无目的地在这些尘土飞扬的垃圾堆中翻找。贝纳拉找到了一把被火焰微微烧黑的曲剑，开始擦拭它以阅读剑刃上的铭文。瓦斯特自娱自乐，手脚并用地打碎脆弱的箱子，想象自己是一个力大无比的巨人。一面破旧的盾牌吸引了泰勒：上面有什么东西激起了歌声回荡。他把它拉出来，把表面擦干净。 “我以前从未见过这个徽章。”贝娜拉从忒伊的肩膀上探过头来。 “我想我见过，但我不记得了。”泰勒低声说，试图唤起他梦中的记忆。他确信他在梦里看到过它。 “看看这个！”瓦斯特喊道，打断了忒伊的思绪。男孩举起了一个水晶球。当他的手在表面移动，拂去砂砾和灰尘时，歌声高了一阶，忒伊浑身颤抖。贝娜拉跑过去看瓦斯特的宝物，忒伊怔在原地。 “你在哪里找到的？”她喘着气，凝视着水晶表面下的漩涡。 “在那辆车上。”瓦斯特指向一堆发黑的木头，除了它的车辐外，几乎无法从其他一堆堆废物中将它辨认出来。贝娜拉开始在半塌的木架中翻找，只有她的脚露了出来。歌声的力量渐渐强大起来，席卷了忒伊全身。他开始慢慢走向瓦斯特。 “把它给我。”他用几乎认不出是自己的声音沉声说。 “不，”瓦斯特小声回道，他的目光定定望着在球体中心反射出来的色彩。“这是我的。” 贝娜拉在车骸中又挖了几分钟，但她没有找到像瓦斯特那样的宝物。里面的大部分东西都已经毁坏，剩下的东西怎么看都平平无奇：断箭、盔甲碎片、瓜尔骨头。她失望地抽出身子回到阳光底下。 忒伊独自一人站在大峡谷边上。 “瓦斯特去哪了？” 忒伊眨了眨眼，然后回过头来，耸了耸肩，笑着对他的堂姐说。“他回去向大家炫耀他的战利品了。你找到什么了吗？” “并没有，”贝娜拉说，“我们也许应该在瓦斯特说漏嘴给我们惹麻烦之前回家去。” 忒伊和贝娜拉开始快步往回走。忒伊知道他们回去时瓦斯特不会在那里。他永远回不了家了。水晶球紧紧地躺在忒伊的书包里，藏在他捡来的一堆杂物下面。他全心全意地祈求歌声复返，淹没峡谷中留下的记忆，淹没那漫长而沉默的坠落。那孩子太过震惊，他甚至来不及尖叫。
